content.plain_english: This week your child is learning about halves, quarters and thirds — splitting a shape, an object or a small group of things into equal parts. They are also discovering that two quarters is the same amount as one half.
content.in_school: Children fold paper to make halves, then quarters, then thirds, and stick the parts back together to see they make a whole. They share groups of objects (for example, 12 counters between 4 children) to find a quarter of a quantity, and write fractions as 1/2, 1/4 and 3/4.
content.home_activity: At lunch, hand your child a sandwich and a piece of fruit. Ask them to cut the sandwich into halves, then into quarters. How many quarters make a whole? How many quarters make a half? Then share a small handful of grapes — say 8 grapes — into 4 equal piles.
dinner_table_questions: If we cut this pizza into 4 equal slices, what fraction is each slice?
Is half of 10 the same as a quarter of 20?
Can you cut your toast into three equal pieces? What is each piece called?

key_vocabulary.definition: A way of showing part of a whole. The bottom number tells you how many equal parts the whole was split into.
One of two equal parts. Written as 1/2.
One of four equal parts. Written as 1/4.
One of three equal parts. Written as 1/3.
Two fractions that have the same value. 1/2 and 2/4 are equivalent — they are the same amount.

example_questions: A chocolate bar has 8 pieces. Sam eats a quarter. How many pieces does he eat?
Show that 2/4 is the same as 1/2 by drawing a square.
content.times_table_tip: Halves come from the 2 times table, quarters from the 4 times table, and thirds from the 3 times table. Knowing those tables makes finding fractions of amounts much faster.
